text:
IMPARTIAL NEWS + INTELLIGENT DEBATE

Account

From a 'ring of steel' around the Seine to RAF anti-drone teams and Qatari armoured vehicles, a vast operation is under way

With days to go before the opening ceremony ofParis 2024, residents and tourists alike this week got their first discomfiting taste of the extraordinary measures needed to make theOlympic Gamesa reality. In the early hours of Thursday, a vast swathe of the French capital became the subject of a strict week-long security clampdown.

Until next Saturday, anyone wishing to wander along the banks of the Seine river where thepioneering opening ceremonyis planned to take place on 26 July, or simply meander past glories from theEiffel TowertoNotre Dame, will need to produce a pre-authorised QR code and undergo a stringent search to pass through a security cordon. All vehicles, with the exception of those belonging to the emergency services, will be more or less banned from the security zone.

Parisians, not generally known for their forbearance of state interference in daily life at the best of times, are unimpressed. The head of the body representing the Paris region hospitality industry complained volubly last week of a “disastrous” intervention which “means you will have to show papers even to drink a coffee”.

The SILT, as the “belt of steel” zone extending up to half a kilometre from the Seine to protect the procession that will form the spectacular opening of the Games is known, is just one example of the vast and intricate security operation being put in place at a cost of €320m (£269m) to protect the10,500 athletesand estimated 16 million visitors – among them at least 500,000 Britons – attending the Paris Olympics.

Over the course of the four-week extravaganza, by some distance the world’s largest sporting event, between 30,000 and 45,000 police and soldiers will be on duty each day. By way of comparison, the total number of police on the streets of London during the2012 Olympicswas 9,500.

The French operation will be bolstered by an array of AI-supported surveillance technology and foreign security specialists, including nearly 250 British law enforcement and military personnel which will include search dog teams and a contingent of Royal Air Force counter-drone experts and equipment.

It is a gargantuan effort which, according to security experts and intelligence sources, is entirely – and soberingly – necessary.

Terrorism has long been identified as one of the key threats to the Paris Olympics. It sits atop adevil’s brew of other menacesto the Games ranging from Russian cyber attacks and disinformation campaigns through to the threat of strikes and protests amid the aftermath ofFrance’s divisive electionswhich have pitched the country into political turmoil.

But the re-emergence in recent weeks and months of Islamist terrorism, most dauntingly in the shape of the Islamic State attack on aMoscow concert hallin March which left 145 dead, and a broader upsurge in geo-political violence – whether it be the Ukraine war, the Israeli bombardment of Gaza or even last week’s assassination attempt onDonald Trump– has added to concern that the Olympics are a prime target for an atrocity.

In June, theIslamic Stateterror group posted an image to its followers featuring an armed drone flying into the Eiffel Tower with a slogan: “Lone wolves’ Olympics have begun.”

To date, the French authorities have foiled what they said were at least three potential plots by lone individuals to attack the Games, including a Chechen teenager suspected of targeting an Olympics football game and an 18-year-old far-right extremist who was arrested by counter-terror officers on Wednesday after he made threats on a neo-Nazi social media chat group.

One Western security official toldithis week: “Anything which chips away at stability in Europe or America is right now a priority for a wide range of threat actors. The Paris Olympics is a prominent symbol in that regard and there are many, many groups out there – from extremists to nation states – who would really like to damage it.

“In order to protect [the Games], you need to mount the sort of security operation that has similar intensity to being on a war footing. The threat is very real, but France has among the best security apparatus available. They will be ready.”

It is not a sentiment shared entirely, it would seem, by the French themselves.

A survey published this week by Paris-based pollsters Odoxa found that 64 per cent of French people believe that security for the Games is “uncertain” and half would like to see the Seine procession, due to be attended by 300,000 spectators, abandoned in favour of a single-venue parade centred around the Eiffel Tower. The places considered by the public to be most vulnerable to a terror attack over the next month are tourist attractions, the public transport network and Olympic fan zones.

Indeed, it is the sheer scale of the Olympics – the Paris Games will encompass 36 locations in France, plus the surfing competition in Tahiti – that makes it such a complex task to protect, with tight security around venues viewed as being likely to deflect any attackers towards softer targets.

As a recent briefing by S&P Global, a data and risk analysis giant, put it: “The high level of security in place at and around the Olympic venues is likely to motivate individuals or small cells intent on conducting an attack during the Games to attempt to target other locations, including fan zones, queues outside of venues, hotels, restaurants and transport hubs.”

From the near doubling of public transport fares to the irritations of needing a QR code to go for a walk along the Seine, the Paris Olympics is proving less than a hit with some Parisians and tourists alike.

The decision by the Paris transport authority to hike fares on the Métro from €2.15 (£1.81) for a single journey to €4 (£3.36) for the duration of the Games is just one hindrance among several that have rubbed the citizens of the City of Light up the wrong way.

The Games Pass, an electronic authorisation required for residents and visitors alike to enter a sprawling zone around the banks of the Seine for the next week ahead of the opening ceremony on 26 July, has led to grumbles that it will drive tourists away and impede Parisians going about their daily business.

One TikTok video by a French-American creator urging people to stay away from Paris during the Games – “Don’t come, cancel everything” – has now garnered more than 760,000 views.

There are increasing concerns that many have heeded such advice. After a spike in hotel prices, room rates have fallen dramatically from €523 (£440) to €282 (£237) a night for a three-star room during the Games, and €738 (£621) to €430 (£362) for four-star lodgings.

Meanwhile, Air France-KLM has reported a dip in revenues of €180m (£151) for July and August with large numbers of seats unsold. Galeries Lafayette, a landmark Parisian department store, has said it expects its revenues to be down by 10 per cent for August.

That said, London experienced a similar drop in tourism numbers during the 2012 Olympics and the Paris authorities insist a good time will be had by those who come to – or remain in – the French capital.

As one local organising committee official put it recently: “It’s going to be magic.”

Perhaps unsurprisingly, the watchword for those involved with ensuring security at the Games is therefore vigilance.

Team GB, which is sending 327 athletes to the Games to compete in 24 sports, will be protected by a security team of private contractors, many of them former Metropolitan Police Service close protection officers, who liaise closely with both the French and UK authorities. It is understood that no threat specific to the British team has been identified.

The British Olympic Association said it had “complete confidence” in the ability of the French authorities and the Games’ organisers to provide a safe environment for both athletes and fans. A spokesperson said: “Our own security function is working closely with domestic authorities, and we have a good understanding of their plans in this critical area.”

In a world of rapid technological advance, these plans include the deployment of a hefty array of cutting-edge equipment designed to monitor, detect and ultimately thwart terrorism threats.

France has passed laws allowing the temporary deployment of AI technology which will sift footage from CCTV cameras in real time to detect pre-determined “events” such as crowd surges, an individual dropping or leaving a bag, the identification of weapons or even a speeding vehicle that could signal a ramming attack.

Earlier this month, French Prime Minister Gabriel Attal reportedly signed off a classified decree allowing the country’s intelligence services to increase surveillance activities – from phone tapping to tracking individuals – by a fifth for the duration of the Olympics.

The clandestine activities follow an altogether more public security clampdown on individuals applying to volunteer or work as civilian security guards at the Games.

Officials in Paris revealed last week that some 3,500 Olympics applicants had been rejected by an extensive vetting process. It was found that 130 of those turned away featured on the government’s “Fichier S” watchlist for terror suspects and others considered a threat to national security.

The French security effort is also being reinforced by 1,750 police and other personnel from 40 countries.

Much to the bemusement of some Parisians – including at least one official in the French interior ministry, a phalanx of Qatari armoured vehicles paraded around the Arc de Triomphe this week after the Gulf state agreed to supply foot patrols and explosives detection specialists to Paris.

The UK is sending a contingent of some 245 police, including special constables to offer guidance to UK visitors on the streets of Paris and sniffer dogs to help with searches at venues. The Ministry of Defence said that it was also sending search dogs as well as RAF personnel and equipment to “help counter potential unauthorised drones”.

The result is what one security expert described as a “maximum effort, with an amount of crossing of fingers” operation to ensure Paris 2024 is remembered for all the right reasons.

As one former Scotland Yard close protection specialist put it: “There are too many variables with the Olympics to offer guarantees… But the truth is, huge amounts of planning and effort go into these events and, overwhelmingly, they pass off without incident. If there is a small amount of inconvenience, then surely that is a price worth paying.”

Impartial news + intelligent debate

All rights reserved. © 2026 Associated Newspapers Limited.
title: 'Terrorism threat is very real': Inside Paris Olympics' £269m security operation
author: Cahal Milmo
description: From a 'ring of steel' around the Seine to RAF anti-drone teams and Qatari armoured vehicles, a vast operation is under way
